title: Red Wine on Silk
stainChemistry: Red wine anthocyanins and tannins bond to silk fibroin through hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions with silk's amino acid groups. Silk dyes are notoriously unstable — many are acid dyes that bond ionically to the silk fiber. The acids in red wine (tartaric, malic) can interact with these dyes, causing color shifts or bleeding. The tannins also have affinity for protein fibers, making red wine one of the most difficult stains to remove from silk without professional intervention.
whyThisWorks: Immediate cold water dilution limits the anthocyanin's ability to bond before it oxidizes. NSD maintains neutral pH and avoids the alkaline conditions that would damage silk's protein structure. The treatment window for silk red wine is very narrow — the longer it sits, the deeper the chromophore bonds and the more likely dye interaction becomes.
defaultAssumption: Treat as urgent. Even a 30-minute delay significantly reduces success rate on silk. This is one of the highest-risk fabric/stain combinations in textile care.

steps: 1. Blot immediately and continuously — maximum absorption before any treatment
2. Flush gently with cold water from back of fabric
3. Apply NSD (neutral synthetic detergent, pH 6-7) — work in very gently with fingertip
4. Cold water rinse — blot with clean white cloth
5. If stain remains: apply tannin spotter briefly (1-2 minutes maximum) — pH must stay neutral
7. Do NOT apply hydrogen peroxide or bleach on colored silk — risk of dye damage
8. On white silk only: very dilute hydrogen peroxide (1%) with strict monitoring — rinse immediately
9. Lay flat to dry — never hang
warnings: NEVER use chlorine bleach — dissolves silk
NEVER use alkaline detergent — breaks silk protein bonds
NEVER use full-strength tannin spotter without dilution — pH too low for silk
NEVER rub or agitate — breaks silk fibers
Silk dyes are unstable — always test on hidden seam before applying anything
Professional treatment is strongly recommended for any dried stain

steps: 1. Blot immediately with clean white cloth — absorb as much as possible
2. Flush very gently with cold water
3. One drop of baby shampoo in cold water — apply to stain, work in with fingertip only
5. Lay flat on clean white towel — do not hang
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop immediately if the silk shows any color change, water spotting, or texture difference. Take to a silk specialist. Do not attempt further treatment at home.
warnings: No OxiClean, hydrogen peroxide, or any bleach on colored silk
Home treatment has limited effectiveness — professional treatment significantly better for red wine on silk

neverDo: Extended tannin spotter without pH control
fiberSensitivities: Silk pH range is 4.0–8.0 — anything outside this damages the fiber
Silk dyes are typically acid dyes — wine acids can cause dye bleeding
Silk fibroin is a protein — any alkaline or enzyme treatment carries fiber damage risk
Water spots are common on silk — always work with minimal moisture

escalation.when: Any dried stain, any stain with color change, or any stain that remains after gentle cold NSD treatment
escalation.whatToTell: Red wine on silk — tannin stain. Possibly affecting the dye. Ask for neutral tannin spotter treatment with strict pH control. No chlorine bleach, no alkaline treatment, no heat.
escalation.specialistType: Silk specialist or certified wet cleaner with silk experience

contextQuestions: Is the silk white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh red wine on silk: immediate cold water + NSD is most effective home option. Dried: professional only. Color change/dye bleeding already visible: professional — may require dye restoration. White silk: slightly more treatment latitude with dilute H2O2 monitored carefully.
